sentences_input.text: In Funfteichen, I was there a year and a few months.
Except that there was a lot of beating going on there, too.
This-- this Sturmbannfuhrer Schamal was a sadist.
He used to-- he used to grab people by the shirt and-- and pull towards you, and then let it loose and used to hit you with the elbows in your face.
So that Schamal knocked four teeth in my mouth, knocked out four teeth.
Also, he would take a shovel, like a spade, and he would hit you on-- in the back of your calf, legs.

I was doing something, I was carrying something, and I did not-- you had to take off your hat and-- like a salute and look-- look-- look him in-- in the eyes-- and I was carrying something in camp and I-- could have been a bale of hay or something.

I was doing a special commander work, and-- and-- and every time, so he just get up, so one time when he hit me like that with the right elbow, and then when I got up he did the same thing with the left elbow.
And I was bleeding.
And he says, this is better than a bullet, because a bullet costs money.
He mumbled in-- to me in German, and I understand German, so I know exactly what he was talking.
And another time I was doing some work and he hit me in the back of my calves, on the leg.
And he used to-- that-- that hurts quite a bit.
And I've seen-- I was lucky enough-- because I've seen him where he would hit people with a shovel, on your throat, or on your head.
And when you fell down, he would take the shovel and place it across your neck and step on it on both sides.
This I have something new for me, a new title, and I've seen him do this quite a few times.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He was the Report Fuhrer and a Sturmbannfuhrer in charge of the inner-- inner-- in the-- like the fuhrer of the concentration camp inside-- not for daily operations.

And he-- he would-- he would walk away from it, and it wouldn't even like-- wouldn't phase him, it wouldn't bother him, and maybe look for another, the next person to receive the same kind of a treatment.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And this was a daily routine concentration camp, a working concentration camp.
Food was always a problem, never enough food.
Cold was a problem, and the conditions were at least a little better than Auschwitz.
And we-- I was there for a year and a few months.
Oh, because my friends always were there to-- to help me out, because I-- I would not go to the infirmary.
I-- at one time I was terribly sick, and I was in-- in Funfteichen.
I think I must have had typhoid fever, and-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and there was a doctor from my hometown that was working in the infirmary. but I went to see him.
But not as a-- I didn't want to stay there because they constantly used to take the people and clean out the infirmary, and send them to--
To the gas chamber, but we did not-- I mean, to the-- to the crematoriums, but in Funfteichen, they would just transport their bodies somewheres to another camp, maybe Gross-Rosen.
Because maybe-- there were-- there were a lot of camps in this area because this was in the-- in the Silesian part of Germany.
So--
Yeah, and he said to me, this pill-- is-- this is the only pill I have left, and these-- these are hard to come by and I'm gonna give you this pill.
And this will make you completely healthy.
I think it was power of suggestion.
I wanted so hard to get well that I-- I recouped from it.
I-- I also had like a little looking glass, a mirror.
I don't know where I got it, but I used to keep it under my bed.
And I used to look at it, and I must have been delirious from the temperature, you know, the high temperature, and it seemed to me like my face is getting smaller every time I looked at it.
But I was very fortunate because maybe I-- I heal fast and that kept me alive, because they constantly transported people out of the camp-- dead bodies, not people.
Because this camp did not have a crematorium.

But the smell was always prevalent no matter what, because the conditions and the sanitary facilities were very minimum, and limited, and-- so-- but it was like, not-- not-- not enough to live and not enough to die.
